news_summary: Innovative solar technology boosts African farmers' incomes, advanced NASA research enhances understanding of environmental challenges, and landmark climate rulings promote accountability and practical solutions for a sustainable future.
news_summary_fr: Une technologie solaire innovante permet d'augmenter les revenus des agriculteurs africains, des recherches de pointe menées par la NASA permettent de mieux comprendre les défis environnementaux, et des décisions judiciaires historiques en matière de climat favorisent la responsabilisation et la mise en place de solutions concrètes pour un avenir durable.
news_summary_es: La tecnología solar innovadora aumenta los ingresos de los agricultores africanos, las investigaciones avanzadas de la NASA mejoran la comprensión de los retos medioambientales y las sentencias históricas en materia climática fomentan la rendición de cuentas y las soluciones prácticas para un futuro sostenible.

articles.title: Solar fridges lift African farmers’ incomes by 50 percent
articles.summary:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM Up to 40 percent of food produced in Africa is lost between harvest and market. Not from drought or pest damage, but from the absence of one thing: refrigeration. The early numbers from solar-powered cold storage are hard to argue with. Provider Soko Fresh reports cutting spoilage rates from […]
The post Solar fridges lift African farmers’ incomes by 50 percent first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News.
articles.content:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM

Up to 40 percent of food produced in Africa is lost between harvest and market. Not from drought or pest damage, but from the absence of one thing: refrigeration.

The early numbers from solar-powered cold storage are hard to argue with. Provider Soko Fresh reports cutting spoilage rates from as high as 50 percent to under two percent. Farmers using the service earn up to 50 percent more per kilogram of produce. In a sector where margins are thin and infrastructure failures are routine, that’s not a small shift.

The structural problem

Cold chains, the network of refrigerated storage, transport, and handling that keeps food fresh from farm to market, are well established in countries like the US, the Netherlands, Japan, and China, where fresh produce stays marketable for weeks. In rural Africa, it can spoil within days.

Conventional refrigeration requires reliable electricity, which most rural areas lack. Diesel generators work, but they’re expensive and emission-heavy. So farmers must sell immediately after harvest, at whatever price buyers will offer, with no leverage and no option to wait.

“Cold storage remains one of the missing links in Africa’s agricultural value chains,” says Emmanuel Aziebor, regional director for Africa at CLASP, a nonprofit supporting energy-efficient appliance deployment. “When farmers can store produce for longer, they gain access to better markets, reduce waste and increase incomes.”

How solar cold rooms work

Off-grid solar cold rooms operate without grid power or fuel costs. Most providers charge farmers per kilogram stored rather than requiring them to buy equipment outright; a standalone unit runs roughly $30,000 (about 25,000 euros).

The model is now operating across Kenya, Nigeria, Ethiopia, Rwanda, and South Africa. In Nigeria, ColdHubs has installed solar walk-in cold rooms at major agricultural markets, available for daily rental. In Rwanda, the same approach is being used for dairy cooperatives, keeping milk viable long enough to actually collect and sell it. Ethiopia is building cold-chain capacity to support horticultural exports, one of its fastest-growing agricultural sectors.

Kenyan farmer Yvonne Anyonyi Mumiah grows rosemary, basil, and other herbs for European supermarkets. Before solar cold storage, transport delays or extreme heat could wipe out her harvest after all the work of growing it. “You can do everything right on the farm,” she says, “but if the produce is not stored properly, you lose both the product and income.”

Electricity access is not the same as economic opportunity

For decades, development efforts in Africa focused on extending grid access to households. That worked, up to a point. Connecting a household to electricity and giving it the means to earn money from that electricity are different problems, and only the first one got serious attention.

“We have neglected the conversation around how people can turn electricity into opportunity,” Aziebor says. “We keep extending electricity infrastructure, but unless people can use that power productively, the economic benefits never fully materialize.”

Solar cold storage sits alongside a wider push toward productive-use technology: solar irrigation for year-round farming, solar milling so rural communities can process crops near the source rather than selling unprocessed at low margins. The question in each case is the same: can electricity be made to generate income, not just light?

Investment remains the constraint

The technology works. The funding doesn’t.

Commercial investors still treat agricultural projects in emerging markets as high risk, especially where business models haven’t proven out at scale.

“These investors see emerging technologies as high risk because we lack enough proven business models with reliable returns,” says Denis Karema, CEO of Soko Fresh. “That makes funding for our type of projects expensive.”

Carol Koech, vice president for Africa at the Global Energy Alliance for People and Planet, says the question is no longer whether the technology works. It’s whether enough bankable projects can be built to pull in institutional money. Grants and concessional finance are covering near-term gaps, but the commercial case needs more volume before private capital shows up at a meaningful scale.

Did this solution stand out? Share it with a friend or support our mission by becoming an Emissary.
articles.source: The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant real-world impact where solar-powered refrigeration drastically reduces food spoilage for African farmers, increasing their incomes by up to 50%. This addresses a major structural problem in food supply chains in multiple African countries, with detailed context on technology, implementation, and economic benefits. The solution has broad significance for food security, economic empowerment, and sustainable energy use in rural Africa.
articles.personality_title: Solar-powered fridges boost African farmers’ income by 50 percent
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In many parts of rural Africa, up to 40 percent of food is lost between harvest and market because there is no way to keep it fresh. This problem is not caused by weather or pests, but by the lack of refrigeration to stop food from spoiling.

**What happened** – A company called Soko Fresh and others have introduced solar-powered cold storage units that don't need electricity from the grid. These solar fridges have lowered food spoilage from as much as 50 percent to less than 2 percent. Farmers who use these cold rooms earn up to 50 percent more money for their crops. This technology is now used in countries like Kenya, Nigeria, Ethiopia, Rwanda, and South Africa.

**Impact** – This is important because farmers in rural Africa usually have to sell their crops immediately after harvest at low prices, due to no refrigeration and unreliable electricity. Solar cold storage lets them keep food fresh longer, so they can sell it when prices are better and reduce waste. For example, a Kenyan herb farmer named Yvonne said that before solar fridges, her harvest was often ruined by heat or transport delays. Now, she can store and sell her produce more successfully.

**What's next step** – Although the technology works well, funding is still a challenge. Investors find agricultural projects in Africa risky because many business models are not proven at large scale. Experts say more projects need to show steady profits to attract bigger investments. Meanwhile, grants and special financing help keep the programs running.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Solar-powered cold storage is helping African farmers keep more of their food fresh, increasing their income by up to half and opening new economic opportunities.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les frigos solaires augmentent de 50 % les revenus des agriculteurs africains
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Dans de nombreuses régions rurales d'Afrique, jusqu'à 40 % des aliments sont perdus entre la récolte et le marché faute de moyens pour les garder frais. Ce problème ne vient pas du climat ou des parasites, mais de l'absence de réfrigération.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Une entreprise appelée Soko Fresh et d'autres ont installé des chambres froides fonctionnant à l'énergie solaire, sans besoin d'électricité du réseau. Ces frigos solaires ont réduit le gaspillage alimentaire de 50 % à moins de 2 %. Les agriculteurs utilisant ces chambres froides gagnent jusqu'à 50 % de plus pour leurs cultures. Cette technologie est utilisée au Kenya, au Nigeria, en Éthiopie, au Rwanda et en Afrique du Sud.

**Impact** – C'est important car les agriculteurs africains doivent souvent vendre leurs produits juste après la récolte à bas prix, faute de réfrigération et d'électricité fiable. La conservation solaire permet de garder les aliments frais plus longtemps, pour vendre à de meilleurs prix et réduire les pertes. Par exemple, Yvonne, une productrice kényane d'herbes aromatiques, explique que son harvest était souvent perdu à cause de la chaleur ou des retards de transport avant les frigos solaires.

**Prochaines étapes** – Même si la technologie fonctionne bien, le financement reste un défi. Les investisseurs considèrent ces projets agricoles comme risqués car les modèles économiques ne sont pas encore tous prouvés à grande échelle. Les experts disent qu'il faut plus de projets rentables pour attirer des investissements plus importants. En attendant, des subventions et des financements spéciaux soutiennent ces initiatives.

**Message clé en une phrase** – La réfrigération solaire aide les agriculteurs africains à conserver leurs produits plus longtemps, augmentant leurs revenus jusqu'à 50 % et créant de nouvelles opportunités économiques.
articles.personality_title_es: Los frigoríficos solares aumentan un 50 % los ingresos de los agricultores africanos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En muchas zonas rurales de África, hasta el 40 % de los alimentos se pierde entre la cosecha y el mercado porque no hay manera de conservarlos frescos. Este problema no se debe al clima ni a plagas, sino a la falta de refrigeración.

**Qué pasó** – Una empresa llamada Soko Fresh y otras han instalado cámaras frigoríficas que funcionan con energía solar, sin necesitar electricidad de la red. Estos frigoríficos solares han reducido el desperdicio de alimentos de hasta un 50 % a menos del 2 %. Los agricultores que usan estas cámaras frigoríficas ganan hasta un 50 % más por sus cosechas. Esta tecnología se usa en países como Kenia, Nigeria, Etiopía, Ruanda y Sudáfrica.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque los agricultores africanos suelen vender sus productos justo después de la cosecha a precios bajos, por falta de refrigeración y electricidad confiable. La conservación solar permite mantener los alimentos frescos por más tiempo, venderlos a mejores precios y reducir pérdidas. Por ejemplo, Yvonne, una agricultora keniana de hierbas, cuenta que antes sus cosechas se dañaban por el calor o los retrasos en el transporte.

**Próximos pasos** – Aunque la tecnología funciona bien, el financiamiento sigue siendo un problema. Los inversores ven estos proyectos agrícolas como riesgosos porque muchos modelos de negocio no están probados a gran escala. Los expertos dicen que se necesitan más proyectos rentables para atraer inversiones mayores. Mientras tanto, subvenciones y financiamiento especial mantienen estos programas.

**Frase resumen** – La refrigeración solar ayuda a los agricultores africanos a conservar mejor sus alimentos, aumentando sus ingresos hasta en un 50 % y creando nuevas oportunidades económicas.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a vibrant African farm landscape at sunset, featuring stylized solar-powered cold storage units glowing gently with cool blue light amid fields of fresh herbs and produce, with silhouetted farmers carrying baskets of colorful vegetables toward bustling market stalls, symbolizing the transformation of harvests preserved and incomes growing through sustainable solar refrigeration.

articles.title: NASA’s PACE Mission Studies Smoke, Fires
articles.summary: With the North American fire season underway, and a record number of acres already burned nationwide, NASA’s Plankton, Aerosol, Cloud, and ocean Ecosystem (PACE) satellite’s three instruments are observing vegetation precursors to fires, along with plumes of smoke and their movement. This data will help scientists piece together clues that deepen their understanding of wildfires. […]
articles.content:
With the North American fire season underway, and a record number of acres already burned nationwide, NASA’s Plankton, Aerosol, Cloud, and ocean Ecosystem (PACE) satellite’s three instruments are observing vegetation precursors to fires, along with plumes of smoke and their movement. This data will help scientists piece together clues that deepen their understanding of wildfires.

“The challenge that we have is to take those clues and use them in a meaningful way, so our models of Earth properly represent what’s happening,” said Kirk Knobelspiesse, a remote sensing scientist working on the PACE mission at NASA’s Goddard Space Flight Center in Greenbelt, Maryland.

Wisps of smoke coming from fires in multiple provinces and territories in Canada travel over the Great Lakes. This image was taken by the Ocean Color Instrument aboard NASA’s PACE satellite on May 31, 2025. NASA

While the satellite, which launched in February 2024, was designed to study Earth’s ocean and atmosphere, it has an unexpected capability: monitoring changes to vegetation. It can also tell us about burn scars, the charred area of land left behind after a wildfire.

“The PACE satellite observes land too, and does it really well,” said Skye Caplan, terrestrial lead for the PACE mission at NASA Goddard. “There is so much to explore with a new hyperspectral data set.”

The Ocean Color Instrument on board PACE is a hyperspectral instrument, observing the planet in several hundred different wavelengths of visible, near infrared, and ultraviolet light. This breadth of the spectrum allows it to gather data on the health of plants, such as their state of stress, dryness, and their relative pigment balance, all of which assist in identifying high fire-risk areas. Land managers can use this data to distribute resources to help mitigate fire risk.

This instrument views the entire Earth daily, with more frequent coverage at high latitudes. With this frequency, on clear days, PACE scientists can quickly assess the aftermath of fires, determining the location and span of a burn scar. Areas that have been burned by wildfire often see increased flood and landslide risk. It’s important to identify these high-risk areas and monitor how they evolve through time, Caplan said.

Using wavelengths in the ultraviolet range, the Ocean Color Instrument can also monitor the smoke after a fire, along with information on how high in the atmosphere these particles drift — height plays a role in how far the particles travel and the systems they impact. The instrument, with its ultraviolet data, expands on fire observations from other satellite instruments, such as the Visible Infrared Imaging Radiometer Suite and the Moderate Resolution Imaging Spectroradiometer.

Thick smoke plumes coming from fires raging in multiple provinces and territories in Canada is visible in this image and affecting a large part of the north of the country. This image was taken by the Ocean Color Instrument aboard NASA’s PACE satellite on Aug. 11, 2024. NASA

The other two instruments on PACE, the Hyper-Angle Rainbow Polarimeter 2 and the Spectro-polarimeter for Planetary Exploration one, are rich with information about the composition of aerosols from vastly different regions, said Andrew Sayer, PACE project science lead for atmospheres from the Ocean Color Instrument at NASA Goddard.

By measuring characteristics of light as it reflects off particles in the atmosphere, these two instruments can determine the quantity of these particles, along with their chemical properties, color, size, and shape. Scientists use this information to differentiate smoke from other particulates. Smoke particulates are typically light absorbing — appearing gray, black, or brown in color — and are small in size compared to other aerosols PACE views, such as pollutants and dust.

Data from PACE will help scientists create more accurate wildfire models and simulate future events, said Knobelspiesse, the satellite’s polarimeter lead. “We’ll be able to then look at different scenarios of emissions in the future and see how smoke that's created in one location can impact other parts of the Earth system.”

By Erica McNamee

NASA’s Goddard Space Flight Center, Greenbelt, Md.
articles.reasoning: The article describes NASA's PACE satellite providing detailed, hyperspectral data on vegetation health, wildfire precursors, smoke, and burn scars, which significantly improves understanding and modeling of wildfires. This has broad environmental and public safety implications by aiding fire risk mitigation and disaster response on a large scale.
articles.personality_title: NASA’s PACE satellite tracks wildfires and smoke to improve fire risk understanding
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Every year, wildfires burn large areas in North America, often causing damage to land and air quality. Understanding when and where fires might start helps communities prepare and respond better.

**What happened** – NASA’s PACE satellite, launched in February 2024, is using special instruments to watch plants, smoke, and burned land. It looks at many colors of light to check how healthy plants are and spot areas that might catch fire. It also tracks smoke from fires and measures tiny particles in the air to learn more about fire behavior.

**Impact** – This satellite provides detailed daily pictures of Earth’s land and atmosphere, giving scientists new clues about wildfires. By knowing which plants are stressed and where burn scars are, fire managers can better predict risks and focus efforts to stop fires or reduce damage. Tracking smoke helps understand how fires affect air quality far away.

**What’s next step** – Scientists will use PACE data to build better fire models that show how smoke spreads and how fires might grow. This will help decision-makers plan for future fire seasons and protect people and nature more effectively.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s PACE satellite uses advanced light sensors to improve how we detect, track, and understand wildfires and their effects on land and air.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le satellite PACE de la NASA suit les incendies et la fumée pour mieux comprendre les risques d’incendie
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Chaque année, les incendies de forêt brûlent de vastes zones en Amérique du Nord, causant souvent des dégâts à la terre et à la qualité de l’air. Comprendre quand et où les incendies peuvent démarrer aide les communautés à mieux se préparer.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le satellite PACE de la NASA, lancé en février 2024, utilise des instruments spéciaux pour observer les plantes, la fumée et les terres brûlées. Il analyse plusieurs couleurs de lumière pour vérifier la santé des plantes et détecter les zones à risque d’incendie. Il suit aussi la fumée et mesure les particules fines dans l’air pour mieux comprendre le comportement des feux.

**Impact** – Ce satellite fournit chaque jour des images détaillées de la terre et de l’atmosphère, offrant aux scientifiques de nouvelles informations sur les incendies. En identifiant les plantes stressées et les cicatrices de brûlure, les gestionnaires peuvent mieux prévoir les risques et concentrer leurs efforts pour limiter les dégâts. Le suivi de la fumée aide aussi à comprendre l’impact des incendies sur la qualité de l’air, même loin des feux.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques utiliseront les données de PACE pour créer de meilleurs modèles d’incendie montrant la propagation de la fumée et la croissance possible des feux. Cela aidera à mieux planifier les saisons d’incendie futures et à protéger les populations et la nature.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le satellite PACE de la NASA utilise des capteurs avancés pour améliorer la détection, le suivi et la compréhension des incendies de forêt et de leurs effets sur la terre et l’air.
articles.personality_title_es: El satélite PACE de la NASA rastrea incendios y humo para mejorar la comprensión del riesgo de incendios
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Cada año, los incendios forestales queman grandes áreas en América del Norte, causando daño a la tierra y la calidad del aire. Saber cuándo y dónde pueden comenzar los incendios ayuda a las comunidades a prepararse mejor.

**Qué pasó** – El satélite PACE de la NASA, lanzado en febrero de 2024, usa instrumentos especiales para observar plantas, humo y tierras quemadas. Analiza muchos colores de luz para revisar la salud de las plantas y detectar zonas con riesgo de incendio. También sigue el humo y mide partículas pequeñas en el aire para entender mejor cómo se comportan los incendios.

**Impacto** – Este satélite ofrece imágenes detalladas diarias de la tierra y la atmósfera, dando a los científicos nuevas pistas sobre los incendios. Al saber qué plantas están estresadas y dónde hay marcas de quemaduras, los encargados pueden predecir mejor los riesgos y enfocar esfuerzos para reducir daños. Seguir el humo ayuda a comprender cómo los incendios afectan la calidad del aire, incluso lejos del fuego.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos usarán los datos de PACE para crear mejores modelos de incendios que muestren cómo se extiende el humo y cómo pueden crecer los incendios. Esto ayudará a planificar mejor las futuras temporadas de incendios y proteger a las personas y la naturaleza.

**Resumen en una frase** – El satélite PACE de la NASA utiliza sensores avanzados para mejorar la detección, seguimiento y comprensión de incendios forestales y sus efectos en la tierra y el aire.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a vast northern landscape showing layered translucent waves of colorful light beams in visible, ultraviolet, and infrared hues sweeping over forests and lakes, symbolizing satellite spectral data; wisps of soft smoke gently drifting above charred burn scars on the land below, with stylized, glowing satellite instruments orbiting overhead emitting subtle rays that highlight vegetation health and atmospheric particles, all portrayed with natural, muted earth tones and soft blues to evoke hope and scientific insight.

articles.title: TotalEnergies ordered to account for client emissions in landmark climate ruling
articles.summary: In a landmark ruling, a French court has ordered energy giant TotalEnergies to account for its clients' emissions in the company's climate plan. It is the first time France's Corporate Duty of Vigilance law has been applied to climate change. FRANCE 24's Sharon Gaffney speaks to Justine Ripoll from Notre Affaire à Tous, one of the NGOs that brought the litigation to court.
articles.content:
TotalEnergies ordered to account for client emissions in landmark climate ruling

France

To display this content from YouTube, you must enable advertisement tracking and audience measurement. Accept Manage my choices One of your browser extensions seems to be blocking the video player from loading. To watch this content, you may need to disable it on this site. Try again Cover image: A PROPOS © FRANCE 24 10:24

10:24 min Share

From the show

Reading time 1 min

In a landmark ruling, a French court has ordered energy giant TotalEnergies to account for its clients' emissions in the company's climate plan. It is the first time France's Corporate Duty of Vigilance law has been applied to climate change. FRANCE 24's Sharon Gaffney speaks to Justine Ripoll from Notre Affaire à Tous, one of the NGOs that brought the litigation to court.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a landmark legal ruling requiring a major energy company to include client emissions in its climate plan, applying corporate responsibility laws to climate change for the first time in France. This represents a significant step in climate accountability with broad societal and environmental implications.
articles.personality_title: French court orders TotalEnergies to include client emissions in climate plan
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – TotalEnergies is one of the world’s largest energy companies. In France, a law called the Corporate Duty of Vigilance requires companies to watch over their actions and those connected to them, to avoid harm. Until now, this law had not been used to address climate change.

**What happened** – A French court ruled that TotalEnergies must include the pollution caused by its customers in its climate plans. This means the company has to take responsibility not only for its own emissions but also for the emissions created when people and businesses use the energy it sells. This decision came after a group of non-governmental organizations (NGOs), including Notre Affaire à Tous, brought the case to court.

**Impact** – This ruling is important because it expands how companies are held responsible for climate change. By including client emissions, it pushes big companies to think about the full impact of their products, not just their own factories or offices. This could lead to stronger actions to reduce pollution and help fight climate change more effectively.

**What's next step** – TotalEnergies will need to change its climate strategy to meet this new legal requirement. Other companies in France and possibly beyond might also face similar demands. The decision could encourage more legal actions that hold companies accountable for all emissions linked to their business.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A French court has set a new rule requiring TotalEnergies to take responsibility for the pollution caused by its customers, marking a key step in climate accountability.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un tribunal français ordonne à TotalEnergies d'inclure les émissions de ses clients dans son plan climat
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – TotalEnergies est l'une des plus grandes entreprises énergétiques mondiales. En France, une loi appelée Devoir de Vigilance des entreprises oblige les sociétés à surveiller leurs actions et celles liées à elles pour éviter tout dommage. Jusqu'à présent, cette loi n'avait pas été utilisée pour le changement climatique.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Un tribunal français a décidé que TotalEnergies doit inclure la pollution causée par ses clients dans ses plans climatiques. Cela signifie que l'entreprise doit prendre en compte non seulement ses propres émissions, mais aussi celles générées lorsque les gens et les entreprises utilisent l'énergie qu'elle vend. Cette décision fait suite à une action en justice lancée par plusieurs ONG, dont Notre Affaire à Tous.

**Impact** – Ce jugement est important car il élargit la responsabilité des entreprises face au changement climatique. En incluant les émissions des clients, cela pousse les grandes entreprises à réfléchir à l'impact total de leurs produits, pas seulement à leurs propres usines ou bureaux. Cela pourrait conduire à des actions plus fortes pour réduire la pollution et mieux lutter contre le changement climatique.

**Prochaine étape** – TotalEnergies devra modifier sa stratégie climatique pour respecter cette nouvelle obligation légale. D'autres entreprises en France et peut-être ailleurs pourraient aussi être concernées. Cette décision pourrait encourager plus d'actions en justice pour tenir les entreprises responsables de toutes les émissions liées à leur activité.

**En une phrase** – Un tribunal français impose à TotalEnergies de prendre en compte la pollution causée par ses clients, marquant une étape clé dans la responsabilité climatique.
articles.personality_title_es: Tribunal francés ordena a TotalEnergies incluir emisiones de sus clientes en su plan climático
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – TotalEnergies es una de las mayores empresas energéticas del mundo. En Francia, una ley llamada Deber de Vigilancia Corporativa exige que las empresas supervisen sus acciones y las relacionadas para evitar daños. Hasta ahora, esta ley no se había aplicado al cambio climático.

**Qué pasó** – Un tribunal francés dictaminó que TotalEnergies debe incluir la contaminación causada por sus clientes en sus planes climáticos. Esto significa que la empresa debe responsabilizarse no solo por sus propias emisiones, sino también por las que se generan cuando personas y empresas usan la energía que vende. Esta decisión llegó después de que un grupo de organizaciones no gubernamentales (ONG), incluyendo Notre Affaire à Tous, llevara el caso a la corte.

**Impacto** – Este fallo es importante porque amplía la forma en que se responsabiliza a las empresas por el cambio climático. Al incluir las emisiones de los clientes, obliga a las grandes empresas a considerar el impacto total de sus productos, no solo sus propias fábricas u oficinas. Esto podría impulsar acciones más fuertes para reducir la contaminación y combatir el cambio climático de manera más efectiva.

**Próximo paso** – TotalEnergies tendrá que cambiar su estrategia climática para cumplir con este nuevo requisito legal. Otras empresas en Francia y posiblemente en otros lugares también podrían enfrentar demandas similares. La decisión podría motivar más acciones legales que responsabilicen a las empresas por todas las emisiones vinculadas a su negocio.

**Una frase clave** – Un tribunal francés exige que TotalEnergies asuma la responsabilidad por la contaminación causada por sus clientes, marcando un paso importante en la rendición de cuentas climática.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a large, sturdy oil rig gently transforming into a flourishing green tree whose branches extend to envelop and connect with smaller glowing orbs symbolizing client emissions, set against a soft, warm sky with natural earth tones, conveying responsibility and interconnected climate care.

articles.title: 'Living in a warming world requires practical solutions that save lives, don't harm our environment'
articles.summary: As Europe confronts increasingly frequent and intense heatwaves, the conversation around climate adaptation is shifting from environmental policy to public health, education, and social infrastructure. Haxie Meyers-Belkin is pleased to welcome Caradee Wright, Chief Specialist Scientist at the South African Medical Research Council leading the Climate and Health Research Programme. She argues that schools have become one of the clearest frontlines of climate change. Her analysis moves beyond the immediate debate over air conditioning to present a more systemic vision of heat resilience, combining architecture, behavioural adaptation, public awareness, and educational policy.Rather than framing extreme heat as a temporary disruption, Wright treats it as a defining condition of the coming decades that demands structural change. She highlights children's unique physiological vulnerability, questions whether historic school buildings remain fit for a warming climate, and argues that protecting education requires redesigning both physical spaces and institutional routines.
articles.content: As Europe confronts increasingly frequent and intense heatwaves, the conversation around climate adaptation is shifting from environmental policy to public health, education, and social infrastructure. Haxie Meyers-Belkin is pleased to welcome Caradee Wright, Chief Specialist Scientist at the South African Medical Research Council leading the Climate and Health Research Programme. She argues that schools have become one of the clearest frontlines of climate change. Her analysis moves beyond the immediate debate over air conditioning to present a more systemic vision of heat resilience, combining architecture, behavioural adaptation, public awareness, and educational policy.Rather than framing extreme heat as a temporary disruption, Wright treats it as a defining condition of the coming decades that demands structural change. She highlights children's unique physiological vulnerability, questions whether historic school buildings remain fit for a warming climate, and argues that protecting education requires redesigning both physical spaces and institutional routines.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a significant and practical approach to climate adaptation focusing on public health and education infrastructure, addressing a broad societal challenge with systemic solutions that can save lives and improve resilience to heatwaves.
articles.personality_title: Rethinking schools to protect children from rising heatwaves
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Europe is experiencing more and stronger heatwaves, which are making people rethink how to adapt to climate change. Instead of just focusing on the environment, experts are now looking at how heat affects public health, education, and social systems.

**What happened** – Caradee Wright, a top scientist from South Africa’s Medical Research Council, explained that schools are now key places where the effects of climate change are felt strongly. She says that fixing this problem isn’t just about adding air conditioning but requires a bigger plan. This plan includes changing school buildings, teaching students and staff how to handle heat, raising awareness, and updating school rules.

**Impact** – Wright points out that children are especially sensitive to heat, and many old school buildings were not made for hotter weather. If schools don’t change, students’ health and learning could suffer when heatwaves hit. Her ideas show that protecting education means changing both the physical classrooms and how schools operate during hot days.

**What's next step** – The next step is to redesign schools to be cooler and safer for children, and to create new ways for schools to manage heat. This could mean building better ventilation, changing school schedules, and teaching everyone how to stay safe in extreme heat.

**One-sentence takeaway** – To keep children safe and learning well, schools must be redesigned and prepared for the hotter world ahead.
articles.personality_title_fr: Repenser les écoles pour protéger les enfants des vagues de chaleur croissantes
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L’Europe connaît des vagues de chaleur plus fréquentes et plus intenses, ce qui pousse à revoir la manière de s’adapter au changement climatique. Au lieu de se concentrer uniquement sur l’environnement, les experts s’intéressent désormais à l’impact de la chaleur sur la santé publique, l’éducation et les systèmes sociaux.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Caradee Wright, une scientifique de premier plan du Conseil sud-africain de la recherche médicale, a expliqué que les écoles sont des lieux clés où les effets du changement climatique se font sentir. Elle affirme que la solution ne se limite pas à installer la climatisation, mais nécessite un plan plus large. Ce plan inclut la modification des bâtiments scolaires, l’apprentissage de la gestion de la chaleur pour élèves et personnel, la sensibilisation et la mise à jour des règles scolaires.

**Impact** – Wright souligne que les enfants sont particulièrement vulnérables à la chaleur, et que beaucoup d’écoles anciennes ne sont pas conçues pour un climat plus chaud. Sans changement, la santé et l’apprentissage des élèves pourraient être affectés lors des vagues de chaleur. Ses idées montrent que protéger l’éducation demande de transformer à la fois les espaces physiques et le fonctionnement des écoles pendant les périodes chaudes.

**Prochaine étape** – Il s’agit de repenser les écoles pour qu’elles soient plus fraîches et plus sûres pour les enfants, et de créer de nouvelles façons de gérer la chaleur. Cela peut inclure une meilleure ventilation, des horaires scolaires adaptés et des formations pour rester en sécurité lors des fortes chaleurs.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Pour protéger la santé et l’apprentissage des enfants, les écoles doivent être adaptées et préparées à un monde plus chaud.
articles.personality_title_es: Rediseñar las escuelas para proteger a los niños de las olas de calor crecientes
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Europa está enfrentando olas de calor más frecuentes e intensas, lo que lleva a repensar cómo adaptarse al cambio climático. En lugar de centrarse solo en el medio ambiente, los expertos ahora analizan cómo el calor afecta la salud pública, la educación y los sistemas sociales.

**Qué pasó** – Caradee Wright, científica principal del Consejo de Investigación Médica de Sudáfrica, explicó que las escuelas se han convertido en lugares clave donde se sienten fuerte los efectos del cambio climático. Ella dice que la solución no es solo poner aire acondicionado, sino un plan más amplio. Este plan incluye cambiar los edificios escolares, enseñar a estudiantes y personal a manejar el calor, crear conciencia y actualizar las reglas escolares.

**Impacto** – Wright destaca que los niños son especialmente vulnerables al calor, y muchos edificios escolares antiguos no fueron diseñados para climas más cálidos. Si las escuelas no cambian, la salud y el aprendizaje de los estudiantes podrían verse afectados durante las olas de calor. Sus ideas muestran que proteger la educación significa cambiar tanto los espacios físicos como la forma en que las escuelas funcionan en días calurosos.

**Próximo paso** – El siguiente paso es rediseñar las escuelas para que sean más frescas y seguras para los niños, y crear nuevas maneras de manejar el calor. Esto podría incluir mejor ventilación, cambiar horarios escolares y enseñar cómo mantenerse seguros en el calor extremo.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Para mantener a los niños seguros y aprendiendo bien, las escuelas deben ser rediseñadas y preparadas para un mundo más caluroso.
articles.image_prompt: A warm-toned detailed painting of a sturdy, sunlit school building with open windows and shaded outdoor learning spaces, surrounded by abstract representations of children as gently glowing silhouettes protected by a translucent, cooling architectural canopy, blending natural airflow patterns and soft, flowing shapes symbolizing behavioral adaptation and community resilience.
